species.citation: Merr. , PJS 1(Suppl.) (1906) 179 ; Merrill, EPFP 1 (1923) 82; ≡ Deyeuxia stenophylla Jansen , Acta Bot. Neerl. 1 (1952) 472; ≡ Deyeuxia suizanensis var. stenophylla (Jansen) Veldkamp , Taiwania 38 (1994) 3; Type: Merrill 4839 (PNH, lecto, lost; L, P, isolecto). = Calamagrostis filifolia forma cleistogama Hack. , PJS 1(Suppl.) (1906) 180; Type: Merrill 4537 (PNH, lecto, lost; BO, L, isolecto).
species.distribution: Endemic to the Philippines. LUZON: Benguet, Ifugao, Ilocos Norte, Mountain Province, Nueva Vizcaya, MINDANAO: Davao del Sur.
species.notes: High montane grasslands. P.B. Pelser (18-Jun-22): Veldkamp (1994) recognized three varieties in Deyeuxia suizanensis (i.e. var. chaseana (Bor) Veldkamp, var. suizanensis, var. stenophylla (Jansen) Veldkamp). Two of these were transfered to Pentapogon and recognized at the species level (Peterson et al. 2021): Pentapogon chaseianus (Bor) P.M. Peterson, Romasch. & Soreng and Pentapogon suizanensis (Hayata) P.M. Peterson, Romasch. & Soreng, but Peterson at al. (2021) did not mention the Philippine taxon (Calamagrostis filifolia Merr. and synonyms listed here). It seems likely that it belongs in Pentapogon, but it is presently unclear to me if it should be recognized at the species level. The name Deyeuxia filifolia is occupied by a different species: Deyeuxia filifolia Wedd. 1800-2900m.

Pelser (29-May-2021): It is unclear to me if this species is native to the Philippines, because some resources suggest that it might be (e.g., Way 2014), whereas others indicate that it isn't (e.g., Harlan & De Wet 1969). 0-610m.

species.citation: Vanderyst , Bull. Agric. Congo Belge 13 (1922) 342. var. nlemfuensis. = Cynodon lemfuensis Vanderyst, Bull. Agric. Congo Belge 11 (1920) 121, nom. inval., nom. provis. Notes: Usually erroneously spelt ‘nlemfuensis’. Veldkamp (unpubl. ms.): “Reported for the Philippines with 3 (!) vernacular names for it by 't Mannetje & Jones (1992). No material was seen, however, so it is not clear which of the at least 2 cultivated with varieties occur(s). Similar to C. dactylon, but rhizome absent, taller, blades 4-7 mm wide, spikes up to 13, up to 10 cm long”. Cultivated, not naturalized.
species.distribution: Africa.

species.notes: In and about towns, chiefly planted. Rojo (1999) states that “..in the sense of Dransfield & Widjaja (1995), this species is conspecific with Dendrocalamus asper. However, current observation of the flowers and vegetative characteristics points to Bambusa rather than Dendrocalamus”. L.L.Co: If this is indeed a Bambusa, why was the epithet asper not used? Basionym Bambusa aspera Schult.f. (1830) clearly antedates basionym Gigantochloa merrilliana Elmer (1908). 305-600m.

species.notes: In open wet places and areas beside streams and riverbanks under partial shade, at low and medium elevation; in fertile alluvial clay or loamy soils. J.E.M. Villasis(14-Jul-24): There has been a longstanding confusion between Oryza minuta and O. officinalis, and the two species are sometimes been treated as synonyms. Oryza minuta has been recorded for Australia and various regions of Asia, probably based on specimens of O. officinalis previously being identified as O. minuta. However, Oryza minuta has only been found in Papua New Guinea and the Philippines (Vaughan & Morishima 2003), and populations outside of these areas are most likely misidentified. 33m.

species.citation: Griff. , Not. Pl. Asiat. 3 (1851) 5 ; Borromeo, Philip. Agricultural Scientist 83 (2000) 135; Type: from Bangladesh. = Oryza nivara S.D.Sharma & Shastry , Indian J. Genet. Pl. Breed. 25 (1965) 161 ; Moody, Weeds reported in rice in South and Southeast Asia (1989) 290.
species.distribution: Australia, Bangladesh, Cambodia, China, India, Java, Laos, Malay Peninsula, Myanmar, Nepal, New Guinea, Philippines, Sri Lanka, Sumatra, Taiwan, Thailand, Vietnam. MINDANAO: Bukidnon.
species.notes: In swamps, open ditches, swampy grasslands, deepwater ricefields, at the edges of lakes or ponds, and sites with stagnant or slow, running water; in clay/loam soil and black soil, in full sun. Only known from the shores of Lakes Apo and Napalit in Bukidnon (Labarosa et al. 2020). The two populations are very distinct from each other, with the population on the first lake having longer leaves, culms, and awns than the other population.

Filip., 1st Ed. (1837) 275.
species.distribution: LUZON: Albay (photos), PALAWAN (photos), PANAY (photos).
species.notes: Throughout the Philippines, in most islands and provinces, but nowhere wild, and of prehistoric introduction. In wet, inundated fields under full sun, partially submerged in fresh or brackish water in fertile, loamy soils. Adventitious plants may occur near or around rice-growing areas, but they cannot establish populations in the wild. Cultivated across about 4.8 Mha of paddy fields in the country, the rice plant is enormously variable, and there are many named varieties that occur in the archipelago. Rice.

species.distribution: Africa, China, India, Japan, Java, Lesser Sunda Isls, Myanmar, Nepal, New Guinea, Ryukyu Isls, Taiwan, Thailand, Vietnam. CULION, LUZON: Benguet, Mountain Province, Nueva Vizcaya, Rizal, MINDANAO: Lanao.
species.notes: Probably introduced in the Philippines where found before 1905. Open grassy slopes and banks chiefly at medium elevation, ascending to 1500m.

species.citation: (Mez) Davidse , Monogr. Syst. Bot. Missouri Bot. Gard. 45 (1993) 1258; ≡ Panicum eminii Mez , Bot. Jahrb. Syst. 34 (1904) 135. = Urochloa ruziziensis (R.Germ. & C.M.Evrard) Morrone & Zuloaga , J. Arnold Arbor., Suppl. Ser. 1 (1991) 269 ; Veldkamp, Blumea 41 (1996) 429; ≡ Brachiaria ruziziensis R.Germ. & C.M.Evrard , Bull. Jard. Bot. État Bruxelles 23 (1953) 373.
species.distribution: Africa. LUZON.
species.notes: Widely cultivated. P.B. Pelser (14-Dec-20): I have thus far only seen specimens from plants grown in cultivation, so I here assume that it is not naturalized.

species.citation: (L.) T.Q.Nguyen , Novosti Sist. Vyssh. Rast. 3 (1966) 13 ; Veldkamp, Blumea 41 (1996) 427; ≡ Brachiaria ramosa (L.) Stapf , Fl. Trop. Afr. (Oliver et al.) 9 (1919) 542; ≡ Panicum ramosum L. , Mant. Pl. (1767) 29.
species.distribution: Africa, ?Philippines, Tropical & Subtropical Asia. ?LUZON: ?Bataan.

genera.last_edited: Last edited by Pieter B. Pelser, 30 November 2020

species.citation: (Hayata) Keng f. , Acta Phytotax. Sin. 6 (1957) 357 ; Dransfield & Widjaja, PROSEA 7 (1995) 154; ≡ Sinarundinaria niitakayamensis (Hayata) Keng f. , Techn. Bull. Natl. Forest. Res. Bur. 8 (1948) 1; ≡ Arundinaria niitakayamensis Hayata , Bot. Mag. (Tokyo) 21 (1907) 49 ; Merrill, EPFP 1 (1923) 94.
species.distribution: China, Philippines, Taiwan. LUZON: Benguet, MINDANAO: Bukidnon (photos), MINDORO.
species.notes: In mossy thickets, sphagnum swamps, and open grasslands, 2200-2700m.

species.citation: L. , Sp. Pl., 1st Ed. (1753) 971 ; Merrill, Fl. Manila (1912) 74; Merrill, EPFP 1 (1923) 28. ssp. mays. Notes: Extensively cultivated in most parts of the Philippines, in some islands and provinces a staple article of food. Corn or maize. Cultivated, not naturalized.
species.distribution: America.

species.citation: (Schrad.) Kuntze , Lex. Gen. Phan. (Post & Kuntze) (1903) 600; ≡ Euchlaena mexicana Schrad. , Index Seminum Hort. Acad. Gotting. 1832 (1832) 3. = Euchlaena luxurians Durieu & Asch. , Bull. Mens. Soc. Linn. Paris, 1st Ser. 1 (1877) 107.
species.distribution: C America.
species.notes: Introduced to the Philippines in the late years of the Spanish occupation and again in the early years of the American occupation and was cultivated experimentally for forage purposes. It has not persisted in cultivation, but see Santos (1986).

genera.last_edited: Last edited by Pieter B. Pelser, 20 September 2017

species.citation: (L.) Merr. , PJS 7c (1912) 230 ; Fl. Manila (1912) 85; Merrill, EPFP 1 (1923) 51; Santos, Guide Philip. Fl. & Fauna 4 (1986) 133; Romeroso et al., Asian J. Biol. Life Sc. 10 (2021) 439; ≡ Agrostis matrella L. , Mant. Pl. Altera (1771) 185. = Zoysia tenuifolia Thiele , Linnaea 9 (1835) 309.
species.distribution: Australia, Bangladesh, Borneo, China, India, Indian Ocean, Japan, Java, Korea, Lesser Sunda Isls, Malay Peninsula, Moluccas, Myanmar, New Guinea, Nicobar Isls, Pacific Ocean, Philippines, Ryukyu Isls, Solomon Isls, Sri Lanka, Sulawesi, Sumatra, Taiwan, Thailand, Vietnam. BATAN, CEBU, HERMANA MAYOR, HOMONHON, LUZON: Albay, Bataan, Bulacan, Laguna, Mountain Province, NCR, Rizal, MINDANAO: Agusan del Norte, MINDORO, PALAWAN, PANAY, PANIQUIAN.
species.notes: Originally from sandy beaches, now commonly cultivated as a lawn grass. Also occurring in open places chiefly along or near tidal streams. 50-250m.

species.citation: (Goudsw.) M.Hotta & Kuroki , Acta Phytotax. Geobot. 45 (1994) 71; ≡ Zoysia matrella var. pacifica Goudsw. , Blumea 26 (1980) 172 ; Santos, Guide Philip. Fl. & Fauna 4 (1986) t. 87.
species.distribution: Moluccas, Pacific Ocean. LUZON: NCR, Rizal.
species.notes: Widely cultivated for lawns, tennis courts, golf courses. Throughout the Philippines (Santos 1986).
